retrieved_docs.title: Evaluating And Referring Patients For Outpatient Monoclonal Antibody Therapy For Coronavirus In The Emergency Department (Archived) -- Issues of Concern
retrieved_docs.content: The challenge faced by emergency department practitioners in evaluating and referring patients for this novel therapy is multifactorial. It requires a thorough evaluation of the patient's clinical presentation and underlying risk factors as well as coordination with outpatient centers to provide the treatment. Clinical assessment and decision-making pathway for patients presenting to the emergency department with COVID-19 infection are outlined below.

retrieved_docs.title: Tocilizumab -- Administration -- Adult Dosing, Dosage Forms and Strength
retrieved_docs.content: A clinical study by Salama C. et al treated adult COVID-19 pneumonia patients at a dose of 8 mg/kg IV, similar to treating cytokine release syndrome. Seven days before or during the study, 80% of the tocilizumab cohort and 88% of the placebo cohort received a glucocorticoid (eg, dexamethasone). Also, about 79% of the patients in the tocilizumab or placebo treatment arms received antiviral treatment (eg, remdesivir). [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Evaluating And Referring Patients For Outpatient Monoclonal Antibody Therapy For Coronavirus In The Emergency Department (Archived) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: The pathogenesis of COVID-19 illness occurs in two distinct phases, an early stage characterized by profound SARS-CoV-2 viral replication followed by a late phase characterized by a hyperinflammatory state induced by the release of cytokines such as tumor necrosis factor-α (TNF α), granulocyte-macrophage colony-stimulating factor (GM-CSF), Interleukin (IL) 1, IL-6, interferon (IFN)-γ, and activation of the coagulation system resulting in a prothrombotic state. Antiviral therapy and antibody-based treatments are likely to be more effective if used during the early phase of the illness. Immunomodulating therapies, either alone or in combination with antiviral and antibody-based therapies, may be more effective when used in the later stage to combat the cytokine-mediated hyperinflammatory state that causes severe illness. [1]

retrieved_docs.title: Evaluating And Referring Patients For Outpatient Monoclonal Antibody Therapy For Coronavirus In The Emergency Department (Archived) -- Issues of Concern -- Novel Prognostic Biomarkers for Risk Stratification in the Emergency Department
retrieved_docs.content: Patients with severe clinical disease on presentation to the ED should be hospitalized and do not qualify for neutralizing monoclonal antibody treatment; however, they may be candidates for anti-IL-6 receptor monoclonal antibodies.

retrieved_docs.title: Evaluating And Referring Patients For Outpatient Monoclonal Antibody Therapy For Coronavirus In The Emergency Department (Archived) -- Issues of Concern -- Novel Prognostic Biomarkers for Risk Stratification in the Emergency Department
retrieved_docs.content: A recent study evaluated patients who presented to the emergency department with COVID-19 infection to determine prognostic indicators that predict the risk of hospitalization and oxygen requirement. They reported that a soluble triggering receptor expressed on myeloid cells (sTREM-1) had high prognostic accuracy for 30-day intubation/mortality. They also reported that interleukin (IL) 6 measured at presentation to the ED had high accuracy for predicting 30-day oxygen requirement. Combining respiratory rate and sTREM-1 predicted 30-day intubation/mortality with 94% sensitivity. An IL-6-based algorithm with respiratory rate had 98% sensitivity for 30-day oxygen requirement. They concluded that sTREM-1 and IL-6 could be used as early triage tools in the emergency department to predict patient outcomes in addition to the clinical assessment. Further research regarding these markers is ongoing. [15] Treatment Plan Based on Clinical Presentation and ED Workup [13]

retrieved_docs.title: Best Practices For Administering Monoclonal Antibody Therapy For Coronavirus (COVID-19) (Archived) -- Introduction
retrieved_docs.content: Management of non-hospitalized patients with COVID-19 in the outpatient setting involves triaging the severity of symptoms and the potential for clinical progression. Identifying patients who are at high risk for severe disease is of prime importance, so patients may be offered therapies that can deter their progression to severe disease.

retrieved_docs.title: Tocilizumab -- Indications -- Off-Label Uses
retrieved_docs.content: SARS-CoV-2 (COVID-19) - Emergency Use Authorization (EUA) The Infectious Diseases Society of America recommends treatment with tocilizumab and a glucocorticoid (eg, dexamethasone) in hospitalized adults with declining blood oxygen saturation levels (eg, pulse oximetry ≤94%) and patients on mechanical ventilation or oxygenation with signs of increased systemic inflammation (eg, C-reactive protein). The National Institutes of Health recommends treatment with tocilizumab and a glucocorticoid (eg, dexamethasone) in hospitalized patients with rapidly declining pulmonary function. For example, hospitalized patients who are candidates for intensive care unit (ICU) admission and require mechanical ventilation or oxygenation or hospitalized patients with rapidly declining pulmonary function requiring ventilation or oxygenation with signs of increased systemic inflammation. A clinical trial by Gordon et al observed that ICU COVID-19 pneumonia patients had improved clinical outcomes, including survival with tocilizumab treatment. [4] A clinical trial by Salama C. et al observed that in hospitalized COVID-19 pneumonia patients who were not on mechanical ventilation, tocilizumab treatment reduced the risk of progression to mechanical ventilation or death. However, the drug did not increase patient survival. [5]

retrieved_docs.title: Emerging Variants of SARS-CoV-2 and Novel Therapeutics Against Coronavirus (COVID-19) -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Anti-IL-6 receptor monoclonal antibodies: Interleukin-6 (IL-6) is a proinflammatory cytokine considered the key driver of the hyperinflammatory state associated with COVID-19. Targeting this cytokine with an IL-6 receptor inhibitor could slow down the process of inflammation based on case reports that showed favorable outcomes in patients with severe COVID-19. [108] [109] [110]  The FDA approved three different types of IL-6 receptor inhibitors for various rheumatological conditions (Tocilizumab, Sarilumab) and a rare disorder called Castleman's syndrome (siltuximab). Tocilizumab is an anti-interleukin-6 receptor alpha receptor monoclonal antibody indicated for various rheumatological diseases. The data regarding the use of this agent is mixed. A randomized control trial involving 438 hospitalized patients with severe COVID-19 pneumonia, among which 294 were randomized to receive tocilizumab and 144 to placebo, showed that tocilizumab did not translate into a significant improvement in clinical status or lower the 28-day mortality compared to placebo. [111] Results from another randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial involving patients with confirmed severe COVID-19 that involved 243 patients randomized to receive tocilizumab or placebo showed that the use of tocilizumab was ineffective in preventing intubation or death rate. [112] The REMAP-CAP and RECOVERY trials (not yet published), two large randomized controlled trials, showed evidence of a mortality benefit in patients exhibiting rapid respiratory decompensation. [113] Sarilumab and Siltuximab are IL-6 receptor antagonists that may potentially have a similar effect on the hyperinflammatory state associated with COVID-19 as tocilizumab. Currently, no known published clinical trials support the use of siltuximab in severe COVID-19. Conversely, a 60-day randomized, double-blind placebo control multinational phase 3 trial that evaluated the clinical efficacy, mortality, and safety of sarilumab in 431 patients showed no significant improvement in clinical status or mortality rate. [114] Another randomized, double-blind, placebo-controlled study on sarilumab's clinical efficacy and safety in adult patients hospitalized with COVID-19 is ongoing (NCT04315298 ).

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 5 -- Level 3 -- Community 2214
retrieved_docs.content: NCIt COVID-19 Terminology encompasses a range of concepts relevant to the disease, including the antiretroviral drug ritonavir and the steroidal compound dehydroepiandrosteron. Dehydroepiandrosteron is classified as an anti‑inflammatory steroid, indicating that it belongs to the broader class of anti‑inflammatory steroids and may play a role in modulating inflammatory processes. By incorporating both ritonavir, which targets viral replication, and dehydroepiandrosteron, which can affect inflammation, the NCIt COVID-19 Terminology reflects the dual therapeutic focus on antiviral activity and control of the inflammatory response in the management of COVID‑19.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 33 -- Level 1 -- Community 88
retrieved_docs.content: NRTIs (abacavir, dideoxycytidine, emtricitabine, lamivudine, stavudine, zidovudine) act as chain terminators during viral DNA synthesis and form the backbone of many combination regimens. Additional nucleoside/nucleotide analogues that are not classified as RT inhibitors—brincidofovir, brivudine, remdesivir—exert activity against DNA viruses and, in the case of remdesivir, against SARS‑CoV‑2. The ATC “Other antivirals” group comprises entry inhibitors (enfuvirtide, maraviroc, maribavir, fostemsavir), monoclonal antibodies (ibalizumab, abciximab), and small‑pox‑targeted drugs (tecovirimat, brincidofovir); ibalizumab exemplifies antibody‑based antiviral therapy. Broad‑spectrum agents such as remdesivir may treat diverse viral infections, while ribavirin may be used for prophylaxis. Brincidofovir and tecovirimat are specifically indicated for smallpox. retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 27 -- Level 3 -- Community 1742
retrieved_docs.content: Maravirok, maribavir, moroxydine, fostemsavir, ibalizumab and tiloron are all classified as members of the “Other antivirals in ATC” group within the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) classification system. This categorization indicates that each of these agents possesses antiviral activity but does not belong to the primary antiviral subclasses, placing them together as alternative or supplementary antiviral options. Recognizing these drugs as part of the “Other antivirals in ATC” category assists clinicians in expanding therapeutic choices for viral infections, especially when conventional antivirals are ineffective, contraindicated, or when a broader antiviral strategy is required.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 1 -- Community 237
retrieved_docs.content: The category “Other antivirals in ATC” encompasses a diverse set of antiviral agents, including enfuvirtid, maribavir, fostemsavir, ibalizumab, moroxydine, and tiloron, all of which are classified within this ATC subgroup. In addition to its antiviral activity, ibalizumab is identified as a monoclonal antibody, linking it to the biologic class “monoklonální protilátky.” Similarly, abciximab is explicitly described as a monoclonal antibody. This overlap illustrates that some agents within the “Other antivirals in ATC” group, such as ibalizumab, serve dual roles as both antiviral drugs and monoclonal antibody therapeutics, highlighting the convergence of antiviral pharmacotherapy and targeted biologic treatments in modern patient care.

retrieved_docs.content: The ATC class “Other antivirals in ATC” comprises a diverse group of antiviral agents, including enfuvirtide, maraviroc, maribavir, fostemsavir, ibalizumab, moroxydine, and tiloron. Each of these drugs is classified as a member of this category, reflecting their shared role as antiviral medications that fall outside the primary antiviral subclasses in the Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical (ATC) classification system. This grouping highlights the breadth of therapeutic options available for viral infections and underscores the importance of recognizing these agents as part of the broader antiviral armamentarium in clinical practice.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 0 -- Community 19
retrieved_docs.content: group and is used to treat viral infection; ribavirin, another nucleoside analogue, is noted for preventing viral infection. The ATC category “Other antimycotics for systemic use” comprises the lipopeptide antifungals kaspofungin and micafungin. “Other antivirals in ATC” include bulevirtide (a lipopeptidy compound), enfuvirtide, maraviroc, moroxydine, fostemsavir, ibalizumab, tecovirimat, and tiloron. Bulevirtide’s lipopeptidy structure links it to other lipopeptide agents. Ibalizumab and abciximab are monoclonal antibodies, the latter primarily serving as an antiplatelet agent. Brincidofovir and tecovirimat exhibit activity against smallpox (الجدري), a viral infection. These shared scaffolds and ATC classifications illustrate how chemical structure, mechanism of action, and therapeutic indication intersect, supporting clinicians in selecting appropriate antiviral, antifungal, or antibody‑based therapies while recognizing important contraindications and epidemiologic risk factors.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 2 -- Community 655
retrieved_docs.content: Antivirals for treatment of HCV infections include ribavirin and telaprevir, both of which are employed as therapeutic agents against hepatitis C virus. Remdesivir has been identified as a medication that may treat عدوى فيروسية (viral infection), while ribavirin is also noted for its potential to prevent عدوى فيروسية. Together, these antiviral drugs illustrate the spectrum of strategies used to both treat and prevent viral diseases, underscoring their importance in clinical management of infections such as hepatitis C and other viral pathogens.
